texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Electrodialysis (ED) is an electro-membrane separation process in which cations and anions could be separated, concentrated and puri fi ed from the aqueous solution using the driving force of an electrical potential gradient. The process is simply based on the property of ion permeable membranes to transport ions. Applying a voltage between two electrodes generates the potential fi eld that is required for the transport of these ions having positive or negative charges. The membranes involved in this process are cation and anion exchange membranes. The cations migrate toward the cathode through cation exchange membranes, while the anions migrate toward the anode passing through anion exchange membranes.
texts.text: Electrodialysis (ED) is an electro-membrane separation process in which cations and anions could be separated, concentrated and puri fi ed from the aqueous solution using the driving force of an electrical potential gradient. The process is simply based on the property of ion permeable membranes to transport ions. Applying a voltage between two electrodes generates the potential fi eld that is required for the transport of these ions having positive or negative charges. The membranes involved in this process are cation and anion exchange membranes. The cations migrate toward the cathode through cation exchange membranes, while the anions migrate toward the anode passing through anion exchange membranes.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Simultaneous separation and recovery of lithium and boron from the aqueous solution prepared by dissolving lithium tetraborate using bipolar membrane electrodialysis (BMED) was studied to investigate the e ff ect of applied potential and initial concentrations of lithium and boron as well as co-existing ions in the solution. The results revealed that mass transfer rates of lithium and boron increased with applied voltage up to a maximum limit voltage. Good linear correlation between initial concentrations and transfer rates of lithium and boron was helpful to elucidate the mass transfer mechanism which can be explained by Fick's fi rst law of di ff usion. The permeation of lithium and boron through the membrane were little bit a ff ected by the applied voltage and also by the initial concentrations of lithium and boron. The in fl uence of co-existing sodium or chloride ions on the BMED performance was not observed. The separation e ffi ciencies of lithium and boron were quite high with a BMED performance > 90%. The current e ffi ciency of lithium and boron gradually decreased with an increase in the applied voltage while speci fi c power consumption increased with an increase in the applied voltage.
texts.text: Simultaneous separation and recovery of lithium and boron from the aqueous solution prepared by dissolving lithium tetraborate using bipolar membrane electrodialysis (BMED) was studied to investigate the e ff ect of applied potential and initial concentrations of lithium and boron as well as co-existing ions in the solution. The results revealed that mass transfer rates of lithium and boron increased with applied voltage up to a maximum limit voltage. Good linear correlation between initial concentrations and transfer rates of lithium and boron was helpful to elucidate the mass transfer mechanism which can be explained by Fick's fi rst law of di ff usion. The permeation of lithium and boron through the membrane were little bit a ff ected by the applied voltage and also by the initial concentrations of lithium and boron. The in fl uence of co-existing sodium or chloride ions on the BMED performance was not observed. The separation e ffi ciencies of lithium and boron were quite high with a BMED performance > 90%. The current e ffi ciency of lithium and boron gradually decreased with an increase in the applied voltage while speci fi c power consumption increased with an increase in the applied voltage.

By applying the direct current electric fi eld, the water molecules migrate into the hydrophilic layer where they can be splitted into H + and OH -ions [3]. Consequently, unlike monopolar ED, the BMED process using bipolar membranes can produce simultaneously acid and base solutions.
texts.text: an anion-exchange layer and a hydrophilic interface at their junction. By applying the direct current electric fi eld, the water molecules migrate into the hydrophilic layer where they can be splitted into H + and OH -ions [3]. Consequently, unlike monopolar ED, the BMED process using bipolar membranes can produce simultaneously acid and base solutions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the end of the 1980s, by developing new type commercial membranes so-called bipolar membranes, electrodialysis in combination with bipolar membranes, so-called bipolar membrane electrodialysis (BMED) was developed to simultaneously split an aqueous saline solution into its corresponding acid and base solutions without addition of chemicals [1]. The method of BMED is considered to be sustainable and environmentally friendly [2]. The bipolar membranes are composed of three parts as shown in Fig. 1: a cation-exchange layer,
texts.text: In the end of the 1980s, by developing new type commercial membranes so-called bipolar membranes, electrodialysis in combination with bipolar membranes, so-called bipolar membrane electrodialysis (BMED) was developed to simultaneously split an aqueous saline solution into its corresponding acid and base solutions without addition of chemicals [1]. The method of BMED is considered to be sustainable and environmentally friendly [2]. The bipolar membranes are composed of three parts as shown in Fig. 1: a cation-exchange layer,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nowadays, lithium is an important element in a wide range of applications like Li-ion batteries, carbon dioxide absorbent in life support systems in spacecrafts and submarines, heat transfer medium and/or raw material for synthesis of lithium derivatives [4]. Although lithium has been mainly recovered from salt lakes in South America, lithium can be also recovered from seawater using lithium adsorbents [5 -8], and from geothermal water [9,10]. The selective recovery of lithium was investigated by Hano et al. using solvent extraction with organophosphorus compounds [9] and by Park et al. using ion exchange combined with magnetite and λ -MnO2 [10]. The implementation of conventional ED process was also considered as promising method for lithium recovery from waste LiBr solution [11].
texts.text: Nowadays, lithium is an important element in a wide range of applications like Li-ion batteries, carbon dioxide absorbent in life support systems in spacecrafts and submarines, heat transfer medium and/or raw material for synthesis of lithium derivatives [4]. Although lithium has been mainly recovered from salt lakes in South America, lithium can be also recovered from seawater using lithium adsorbents [5 -8], and from geothermal water [9,10]. The selective recovery of lithium was investigated by Hano et al. using solvent extraction with organophosphorus compounds [9] and by Park et al. using ion exchange combined with magnetite and λ -MnO2 [10]. The implementation of conventional ED process was also considered as promising method for lithium recovery from waste LiBr solution [11].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: level of boron concentration accepted in drinking water is 2.4 mg/L [16]. Considering the increasing boron concentration in surface waters as well as the need for desalination of seawater containing 4 -5 mg/L of boron for production of potable water, the development of e ff ective technologies for the boron removal is desired.
texts.text: level of boron concentration accepted in drinking water is 2.4 mg/L [16]. Considering the increasing boron concentration in surface waters as well as the need for desalination of seawater containing 4 -5 mg/L of boron for production of potable water, the development of e ff ective technologies for the boron removal is desired.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Currently, the processes of evaporation, crystallization or solvent extraction are used in the production of boric acid [17], but suitable only for high boron concentration. The removal of trace amount of boron from aqueous solution is commonly achieved by using the adsorption with chelating ion exchange resins [18]. The membrane processes form the second group of widely used technologies for boron removal from water for applying the desalination of seawater or brackish water. In order to remove residual boric acid by membrane processes, pH has to be raised to 10 -11 to convert boric acid (B(OH)3) to borate (B(OH)4 -) form [19]. Although increasing the alkalinity of the solution enables the boron removal with the e ffi ciency of 98 -99%, the precipitation of the magnesium and calcium hydroxides is deposited on the membrane surface in high pH region by forming membrane fouling so-called scaling [20,21].
texts.text: Currently, the processes of evaporation, crystallization or solvent extraction are used in the production of boric acid [17], but suitable only for high boron concentration. The removal of trace amount of boron from aqueous solution is commonly achieved by using the adsorption with chelating ion exchange resins [18]. The membrane processes form the second group of widely used technologies for boron removal from water for applying the desalination of seawater or brackish water. In order to remove residual boric acid by membrane processes, pH has to be raised to 10 -11 to convert boric acid (B(OH)3) to borate (B(OH)4 -) form [19]. Although increasing the alkalinity of the solution enables the boron removal with the e ffi ciency of 98 -99%, the precipitation of the magnesium and calcium hydroxides is deposited on the membrane surface in high pH region by forming membrane fouling so-called scaling [20,21].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recently, adsorption-membrane fi ltration hybrid process was suggested as an alternative method for boron removal from geothermal water [22]. Electrodialysis (ED) has also been considered as an alternative method for boron removal [23 -25]. Hiroki et al. reported the possibility to reduce the boron concentration to an acceptable level in solutions of various pHs by using BMED [26].
texts.text: Recently, adsorption-membrane fi ltration hybrid process was suggested as an alternative method for boron removal from geothermal water [22]. Electrodialysis (ED) has also been considered as an alternative method for boron removal [23 -25]. Hiroki et al. reported the possibility to reduce the boron concentration to an acceptable level in solutions of various pHs by using BMED [26].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The present study focused on the investigation of the feasibility of BMED process to separate and recover lithium and boron as co-existing element from geothermal waters. The main key parameters in fl uencing mass transfer coe ffi cients, the e ffi ciencies of separation and recovery of lithium and boron such as initial concentrations of lithium, boron and co-existing ions such as sodium and chloride as well as the applied potential voltage were experimentally studied.
texts.text: The present study focused on the investigation of the feasibility of BMED process to separate and recover lithium and boron as co-existing element from geothermal waters. The main key parameters in fl uencing mass transfer coe ffi cients, the e ffi ciencies of separation and recovery of lithium and boron such as initial concentrations of lithium, boron and co-existing ions such as sodium and chloride as well as the applied potential voltage were experimentally studied.

texts.orig: 2. Experimental
texts.text: 2. Experimental

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Experimental set up
texts.text: 2.1. Experimental set up

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The BMED experiments were carried out using an ACILYZER EX3B (ASTOM Co. Ltd). The BMED stack is completely assembled with 10 three chamber units (CMB cation exchange membranes AHA anion exchange membrane and BP-1E bipolar membranes) and electrodes (anode: Pt/Ir-MMO coated Ti-stretched metal, cathode: stainless steel). The speci fi cations of the membranes were given in Table 1. The e ff ective membrane area was totally 550 cm 2 (55 cm 2 /cell). The fl ow sheet for separation and recovery of lithium and boron was shown in Fig. 2.
texts.text: The BMED experiments were carried out using an ACILYZER EX3B (ASTOM Co. Ltd). The BMED stack is completely assembled with 10 three chamber units (CMB cation exchange membranes AHA anion exchange membrane and BP-1E bipolar membranes) and electrodes (anode: Pt/Ir-MMO coated Ti-stretched metal, cathode: stainless steel). The speci fi cations of the membranes were given in Table 1. The e ff ective membrane area was totally 550 cm 2 (55 cm 2 /cell). The fl ow sheet for separation and recovery of lithium and boron was shown in Fig. 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Model solution of lithium tetraborate (Li2B4O7·5H2O) was used at various concentrations. The lithium concentrations in sample solution were 34, 68, 170 and 340 mg/L and boron concentrations were 100, 200, 500 and 1000 mg/L, respectively. NaCl was dissolved into the model solution for investigating the in fl uence of co-existing ions Na + and Cl -. Applied voltages were 25, 28, 30 and 35 V. The volume of the sample compartment was 2.0 L and those of acid and base compartments were 200 mL of 0.1 mol/LHCl and 0.1 mol/L NaOH, respectively. A 500 mL of 0.1 mol/L NaOH was circulated in the electrode compartment. The BMED system was run for 2 h and sampling from sample, acid and base compartments were made after each 20 min for chemical analyses. The concentrations of lithium, boron and sodium were analyzed by an induced coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-AES, Shimadzu ICPE-9000) and that of chloride was done by an ion chromatograph (Dionex DX120).
texts.text: Model solution of lithium tetraborate (Li2B4O7·5H2O) was used at various concentrations. The lithium concentrations in sample solution were 34, 68, 170 and 340 mg/L and boron concentrations were 100, 200, 500 and 1000 mg/L, respectively. NaCl was dissolved into the model solution for investigating the in fl uence of co-existing ions Na + and Cl -. Applied voltages were 25, 28, 30 and 35 V. The volume of the sample compartment was 2.0 L and those of acid and base compartments were 200 mL of 0.1 mol/LHCl and 0.1 mol/L NaOH, respectively. A 500 mL of 0.1 mol/L NaOH was circulated in the electrode compartment. The BMED system was run for 2 h and sampling from sample, acid and base compartments were made after each 20 min for chemical analyses. The concentrations of lithium, boron and sodium were analyzed by an induced coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-AES, Shimadzu ICPE-9000) and that of chloride was done by an ion chromatograph (Dionex DX120).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Effect of applied voltage and initial concentrations
texts.text: 3.1. Effect of applied voltage and initial concentrations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Simultaneous separation and recovery of lithium and boron was investigated by using BMED system. Fig. 3 shows the time-course variations of lithium concentration in the sample and base compartments. In sample compartment, lithium concentration monotonously decreased with time, while in the base compartment, it increased by time. The decrease rate of lithium concentration was faster by the increase in applied voltage. The fi ndings from this study on the in fl uence of applied voltage on lithium and borate ions removal were similar with those reported in the literature [28].
texts.text: Simultaneous separation and recovery of lithium and boron was investigated by using BMED system. Fig. 3 shows the time-course variations of lithium concentration in the sample and base compartments. In sample compartment, lithium concentration monotonously decreased with time, while in the base compartment, it increased by time. The decrease rate of lithium concentration was faster by the increase in applied voltage. The fi ndings from this study on the in fl uence of applied voltage on lithium and borate ions removal were similar with those reported in the literature [28].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An increase of separation and recovery e ffi ciencies during acid and base production from NaCl and/or RO brine with BMED was also reported [29 -31]. The concentration ratios between initial concentration of lithium in the sample compartment and the fi nal concentration in the base compartment were reached to 5 at 120 min, because of the volume ratio of the solution between the sample and base compartments (2000 mL vs. 200 mL at the initial state).
texts.text: An increase of separation and recovery e ffi ciencies during acid and base production from NaCl and/or RO brine with BMED was also reported [29 -31]. The concentration ratios between initial concentration of lithium in the sample compartment and the fi nal concentration in the base compartment were reached to 5 at 120 min, because of the volume ratio of the solution between the sample and base compartments (2000 mL vs. 200 mL at the initial state).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 4 shows time-course variations of boron concentration in the sample and acid compartments. As same as time-course variations of lithium, the boron concentration in the sample compartment decreased with time, while that in the acid compartment increased with time. The decrease rate of boron concentration was also faster by the increase in applied voltage as monitored in the case of lithium. The concentration ratios between initial concentration of boron in the sample compartment and the fi nal concentration in the base compartment were reached to 6 at 120 min. Therefore, the simultaneous separation and recovery of lithium and boron could be achieved by BMED system.
texts.text: Fig. 4 shows time-course variations of boron concentration in the sample and acid compartments. As same as time-course variations of lithium, the boron concentration in the sample compartment decreased with time, while that in the acid compartment increased with time. The concentration ratios between initial concentration of boron in the sample compartment and the fi nal concentration in the base compartment were reached to 6 at 120 min. Therefore, the simultaneous separation and recovery of lithium and boron could be achieved by BMED system.

texts.orig: Fig. 8 shows time-course variations of co-existing ions, sodium and chloride in the sample compartments. As same as time-course variations of lithium and boron, sodium as a cation was transported to base compartment, while chloride as an anion was transported to acid compartment. These time-course variations are also con fi rmed to be good linear relationships between ln( C / C 0) and t , to obtain the mass transfer coe ffi cient of sodium and chloride, k Na and k Cl .
texts.text: Fig. 8 shows time-course variations of co-existing ions, sodium and chloride in the sample compartments. As same as time-course variations of lithium and boron, sodium as a cation was transported to base compartment, while chloride as an anion was transported to acid compartment. These time-course variations are also con fi rmed to be good linear relationships between ln( C / C 0) and t , to obtain the mass transfer coe ffi cient of sodium and chloride, k Na and k Cl .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Fig. 9 shows the relationship between k Li and initial concentration of sodium ion, C Na0, as a co-existing cation. The k Li are almost constant with increasing C Na0. This suggests that sodium ion does not a ff ect to the ion transfer of lithium, although sodium ion transfer to cation exchange membrane as same as lithium ion.
texts.text: Fig. 9 shows the relationship between k Li and initial concentration of sodium ion, C Na0, as a co-existing cation. The k Li are almost constant with increasing C Na0. This suggests that sodium ion does not a ff ect to the ion transfer of lithium, although sodium ion transfer to cation exchange membrane as same as lithium ion.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Fig. 10 shows the relationship between k B and the initial concentration of chloride ion, C Cl0, as a co-existing anion. The k B are almost constant with increasing C Cl0, although the plots are little bit fl uctuated. This suggests that chloride ion does not a ff ect the ion transfer of boron, although chloride ion transfers to anion exchange membrane as same as borate ion. There was no competition between the transport of borate and chloride. Some co-existing anions such as hydroxyl ions were reported to retard or limit borate ions transport through anion exchange membranes. The transport of borate ions was speeded up after the complete transfer of hydroxyl ions [28]. However, higher preference of hydroxyl ions than borate ions may depend on the permselectivity of
texts.text: Fig. 10 shows the relationship between k B and the initial concentration of chloride ion, C Cl0, as a co-existing anion. The k B are almost constant with increasing C Cl0, although the plots are little bit fl uctuated. This suggests that chloride ion does not a ff ect the ion transfer of boron, although chloride ion transfers to anion exchange membrane as same as borate ion. There was no competition between the transport of borate and chloride. Some co-existing anions such as hydroxyl ions were reported to retard or limit borate ions transport through anion exchange membranes. The transport of borate ions was speeded up after the complete transfer of hydroxyl ions [28]. However, higher preference of hydroxyl ions than borate ions may depend on the permselectivity of

The current e ffi ciency of lithium and boron, ƞ Li and ƞ B, gradually decreased from 22% to 18% for lithium and from 43% to 36% for boron, with an increase in the applied voltage. texts.label: text
texts.orig: applied voltage was due to the increase of total electrical charge Q which is inversely proportional to ƞ (Eq. (4)). It was proven that by working over 30 V, trends of lithium/boron separation dependence with applied potential were not in good linear relationships between ln ( C / C 0) and t. But from 25 V to 30 V, trends of lithium/boron separation dependence with applied potential were con fi rmed to be good linear relationships between ln( C / C 0) and t [32]. This may suggest that working over 30 V leads to working over the limiting current density. The SPC value increased with an increase in the applied voltage, although ƞ Li and ƞ B decreased. Water di ff usion from sample compartment to acid and base compartments was also observed due to the di ff usion of hydrated ions from sample compartment to acid or base compartments. The amount of water di ff used from sample compartment to base compartment was higher than that to acid compartment. This is due to the higher permeation rate of lithium ion rather than that of borate ion.
texts.text: applied voltage was due to the increase of total electrical charge Q which is inversely proportional to ƞ (Eq. (4)). It was proven that by working over 30 V, trends of lithium/boron separation dependence with applied potential were not in good linear relationships between ln ( C / C 0) and t. But from 25 V to 30 V, trends of lithium/boron separation dependence with applied potential were con fi rmed to be good linear relationships between ln( C / C 0) and t [32]. This may suggest that working over 30 V leads to working over the limiting current density. The SPC value increased with an increase in the applied voltage, although ƞ Li and ƞ B decreased. Water di ff usion from sample compartment to acid and base compartments was also observed due to the di ff usion of hydrated ions from sample compartment to acid or base compartments. The amount of water di ff used from sample compartment to base compartment was higher than that to acid compartment. This is due to the higher permeation rate of lithium ion rather than that of borate ion.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Simultaneous separation and recovery of lithium and boron from aqueous solution were investigated by using BMED method. The permeation of lithium and boron through the membrane were little bit a ff ected by applied voltage and also the concentrations of lithium and boron. The in fl uences of co-existing sodium and chloride ions were not observed, although sodium and chloride ions also permeated simultaneously. The separation e ffi ciencies of lithium and boron were quite high (> 90%) and the current e ffi ciency of lithium and boron gradually decreased with increase in the applied voltage, while SPC increased with increase in the applied voltage.
texts.text: Simultaneous separation and recovery of lithium and boron from aqueous solution were investigated by using BMED method. The permeation of lithium and boron through the membrane were little bit a ff ected by applied voltage and also the concentrations of lithium and boron. The in fl uences of co-existing sodium and chloride ions were not observed, although sodium and chloride ions also permeated simultaneously. The separation e ffi ciencies of lithium and boron were quite high (> 90%) and the current e ffi ciency of lithium and boron gradually decreased with increase in the applied voltage, while SPC increased with increase in the applied voltage.
